name: na_type_semantic_divergence
description: NaN, pd.NA, and NaT follow different comparison and propagation semantics that look identical on the surface
seed_rules: `NaN != NaN` returns True because float NaN follows IEEE 754 arithmetic — but `pd.NA != pd.NA` returns `pd.NA` (not True) because pd.NA uses three-valued SQL logic, not float arithmetic. The identical-looking inequality silently changes behavior depending on which NA type the column contains, and this depends entirely on the column's dtype (float64 vs nullable Int64/boolean).

`pd.array([pd.NA], dtype="boolean").any()` can return False while `.all()` returns True on a single-element NA array (confirmed bug #65223) — because the SQL three-valued logic was implemented per-reduction independently, and the boundary case where the array contains only NA values was handled inconsistently. Practitioners who assume `any` and `all` are duals over nullable arrays will get contradictory results.

Checking `if pd.isna(x)` is not equivalent to `x is pd.NA`: `pd.isna(None)` is True, `pd.isna(float('nan'))` is True, and `pd.isna(pd.NaT)` is True — all different Python types. Using `== pd.NA` instead of `pd.isna()` always returns `pd.NA` (not True) because pd.NA propagates through all comparisons by design, so `value == pd.NA` can never be used as a boolean guard.

name: multiindex_loc_dtype_trap
description: MultiIndex .loc/.at silently returns wrong results or raises cryptic errors on cross-dtype key comparisons
seed_rules: On a simple Index, `df.loc["1"]` on an integer Index raises `KeyError` cleanly. On a MultiIndex, `df.loc[("1", ...)]` where the first level is integer silently passes the string "1" to `numpy.searchsorted` against an integer array — which is cross-dtype undefined behavior in numpy — returning wrong positional results without any error (confirmed bug #65230). The MultiIndex fast-path skips the type coercion that the scalar Index path performs.

`df.at[("AMZN", "2021-08-02"), "col"]` on a MultiIndex where one level is DatetimeIndex raised `TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for +: 'slice' and 'int'` (bug #65221) — because `DataFrame._get_value` used an engine fast-path that assumed all index levels were fully resolved, and partial date strings (which need timestamp parsing) caused a slice object to leak into arithmetic. The same partial string key that works on a plain DatetimeIndex breaks silently on MultiIndex.

Slicing a MultiIndex with a `datetime.date` object (not `datetime.datetime`) on a DatetimeIndex level raised `TypeError: Level type mismatch` (bug #65245) — even though the same slice works on a standalone DatetimeIndex with a deprecation warning. MultiIndex level slicing has stricter type-matching than scalar Index slicing because each level's type coercion is implemented separately per index type combination.

name: loc_setitem_value_shape_inference
description: loc/iloc setitem routes assignment through different code paths based on shape inference that misclassifies tuples and nested lists
seed_rules: `df.loc[bool_mask, "col"] = [(1, 2), (3, 4)]` raised `ValueError` on a mixed-dtype DataFrame (bug #65264) because the internal `_is_2d_value` heuristic classified a list of tuples as a 2D array (since numpy treats `[(1,2),(3,4)]` as shape (2,2)), routing to the 2D assignment path where numpy's boolean-indexed assignment unpacked the tuples. The fix added a special-case: lists whose elements are all tuples are treated as a list of scalar tuple objects, not a 2D array.

Assigning a list of tuples vs a list of lists to the same `df.loc[mask, col]` takes fundamentally different internal paths: list-of-lists is correctly treated as 2D (each inner list = a row of values), while list-of-tuples should be treated as 1D (each tuple = a scalar cell value). This distinction breaks down when the DataFrame has mixed dtypes that trigger the block-based code path rather than the array-based path.

`df.loc[:, col] = scalar` on a boolean-masked, mixed-dtype DataFrame can trigger NumPy's broadcasting rules before pandas can apply its own type coercion — meaning the effective dtype of the column after assignment depends on whether pandas handled it in the pandas layer or fell through to a numpy boolean-index assignment, which can silently upcast or fail depending on the scalar type.
